title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the Grafana dashboard showing a consistent daily memory climb is very helpful and strongly suggests a memory leak.

To help us investigate, could you share your environment details? Specifically:
- What version of TaskFlow are you running?
- What OS and runtime is the server on (e.g., Node version, Docker, bare metal)?
- What database backend are you using (and its version)?
- Any reverse proxy or caching layer in front of TaskFlow?

This will help us narrow down where the leak might be occurring.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're running TaskFlow v2.3 — we upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, which is actually right around when this problem started. The server is on Ubuntu 22.04, it's a VM with 8GB RAM running bare metal (no Docker). I don't think we have any special caching layer, just the default setup. As for the database, we're using whatever TaskFlow ships with by default — I'd have to check the exact version. Come to think of it, the timing with the v2.3 upgrade is pretty suspicious. The v2.3 changelog mentioned something about 'improved real-time notifications' — could that be related?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in TaskFlow v2.3 causes progressive server slowdown requiring daily restart","problem":"A self-hosted TaskFlow instance experiences a consistent daily memory leak, climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. This causes page loads to exceed 10 seconds and API timeouts by late afternoon, forcing a daily server restart. The issue affects approximately 200 active users.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The memory leak was introduced in the v2.3 upgrade, most likely in the 'improved real-time notifications' feature. A common pattern: real-time notification systems (e.g., WebSocket connections, SSE streams, or in-memory event listeners) can leak if connections or event handlers are not properly cleaned up when users disconnect or sessions expire. With 200 active users, accumulated leaked connections/handlers throughout the day would explain the steady memory growth.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server (Ubuntu 22.04, bare metal, default database configuration)","Allow normal usage by ~200 active users throughout the day","Monitor memory usage via Grafana or system tools","Observe memory climbing steadily from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over 8-10 hours","Note progressive page load degradation and eventual API timeouts"],"environment":"TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 approximately one week ago), Ubuntu 22.04, VM with 8GB RAM, bare metal (no Docker), default database backend, no special caching layer, ~200 active users","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 active users experience progressively degrading performance throughout the day, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires manual daily server restarts, creating a maintenance burden and end-of-day downtime.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the real-time notifications feature introduced in v2.3. Specifically: (1) Check for WebSocket/SSE connection cleanup on client disconnect, (2) Look for event listener accumulation (missing removeListener/off calls), (3) Check for in-memory caches or subscription registries that grow without eviction, (4) Run the server with --inspect or heap profiling to identify the leaking objects. As a quick verification, compare the notification subsystem code between v2.2 and v2.3.","proposed_test_case":"Create a load test that simulates 200 users connecting, performing actions that trigger notifications, and disconnecting over a simulated 8-hour period. Assert that memory usage remains within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed startup memory + a reasonable threshold) and that all notification-related resources (connections, listeners, subscriptions) are properly freed on disconnect.","information_gaps":["Exact database backend and version (reporter unsure, using defaults)","Server-side error logs or stack traces during the slowdown period","Whether the real-time notifications feature is actively used or could be disabled as a workaround","Heap dump or profiler output identifying the specific leaking objects"]}
